BEARING 315
The Newsletter of Orienteering in the Pacific Northwest
September, 1995
Cascade Orienteering

The Bald Mountain Rogaine

Terradan Landchild
Benevolent Almighty Event Director

CROC's first major "O-vent" was an outstanding success with extensive, detailed planning, deep staff support from several clubs, a well-designed course and map, fantastic food, incredible scenery (sunrise on Mt. Rainier was especially spectacular) and even perfect weather! 73 enthusiastic participants formed 33 teams quite evenly split between the 24- and 12-hour sections. As much work as the staff put into this event, it's the participants who made it happen!
The 1995 North American Championship title went to the Canadian Men's Masters team of Brian Chubb and Allan Stradeski of Kamloops, BC. Second place overall by a narrow margin (but first place in 24M) was a UK team, John Britton and Mark Seddon. John is currently "on loan" to CROC from the Manchester OC. However, the North American Championships were very nearly awarded to a European team! [John Britton writes about their experiences later in this issue. – Ed.]
Complete official results are in the results section of this newsletter. For now, let's applaud those teams who made long trips to attend the Rogaine from: Belgium (with Michel Billaux, currently living in Pasco); yet another UK team (Pete Stagg & Jane Hancock); New York (Trysons); Maryland & VA (Brake & Organek); Louisville, KY (Greenlee & Bruce Moore); Indiana (Eric Grossman & Vlad Gusiatnikov); Idaho (Harbuck & Leveridge); Medford, OR (Joe Hallett & Danny Miller); Pt. Mugu and San Diego, CA (Ron Hudson & John Bennett); Edmonton, Alberta (Don Scott & Gene Brenda) and six more teams from British Columbia including one team of self described "ex-patriate" Australians (Glen Mair & Merideth Dally) who supplied the Canadian and Australian flags!
The course itself was well-designed providing many different route choices; nothing was really obvious. As a safety precaution, all teams left a copy of their planned route on a black and white copy of the course map. There were a total of 48 controls ranging from 20 to 100 points depending upon difficulty, distance and point density. The Britton/Seddon team came closest at 41 to getting them all. Two controls were especially difficult: #32 on a spur and Boulder Cave. Bob is giving credit to all who reported the Cave control "missing"; actually the marker was simply "offset" which we should have noted on the description sheet.
Although all of the participants at the pre-Start briefing loudly expressed their goal of having a fun Rogaine, some were also serious about finding controls! The most competitive teams ("elites") averaged about 100 points per hour, experienced teams, 60 points per hour and beginner teams, 30 points per hour, in both the 12- and 24-hours sections. The 12-hour section was any 12 hours out of the noon Saturday to noon Sunday time period. To gain night navigation experience, the 12-hour teams were also offered a "freebie": they could go out at dark and find one control and return while "off the clock" and not use any of their allotted 12 hours. Four teams took advantage of the offer and enjoyed "real Rogaining". Most were too tired from traversing the rigorous terrain.
I rushed through the staff recognitions at the Awards Ceremony; I meant to put each staff in the spotlight and embarrass them one at a time but didn't. So here they are:

  • Bob Reddick (SAMM): The ROGAINE-MEISTER, who sparked the original inspiration and gave yeoman service as consultant, field-checker, timer and results coordinator, control pick-up.
  • Les Stark (CROC): Course designer, cartographer, field-checker, control setter, water crew chief, control picker, T-shirt design & ordering... (with help from his wife, Beth).

Of course, Less, Bob & I coordinated on dozens of details before the Rogaine! Also, the following folks all pitched in for much more than the jobs I have listed:

  • Mal Harding (CROC): field-checking, control setting & pick-up, traffic control
  • Linda & Carl Moore (NISQ): vetters & course setters
  • Ken Lew (SAMM): Assistant Timer & results
  • Crystal Parks (CROC): traffic control
  • Matt Boser (CROC): water crew
  • Don Atkinson (COC): porta-potty procurement, asst. timer
  • Craig Webster (CROC): photographer, Internet consultant.

Special Appreciation Award to Kent Verbeck (EOC) for the fantastic food service! Not only wonderful food, but also enough quantity to leave once-starving Rogainers groaning when he offered thirds & fourths! I think folks will be talking about Kent's BBQ for years!
Special Appreciation Award to my lovely wife, Jill Miller (CROC) for her infinite patience with an anxious, inexperienced and nuts Event Director during this past year of planning and development. Jill also waited on me hand and foot all during the event, finding things I had misplaced, making sure I ate and slept and making it possible for me to focus! Jill re-learned lashing and constructed the washstands and pitilators and did the horrible job of attaching reflective strips onto the CROC control markers with that nasty water-proof glue! I also appreciate her sacrifice of her vacation time to help me set up camp the week before the event, include what should have been our 1st Anniversary honeymoon trip!
Thanks also to additional field-checkers Dave Tallent & Debbie Newell (COC).
Do you notice the "cross-fertilization effect"? The Rogaine was officially a joint effort of the CROC and SAMM clubs, but members of EOC, NISQ, and COC were also on staff. It just goes to show what cooperation can do for you!
Thanks also to our sponsors REI, PowerBar, BioZone, and the Naches District, Wenatchee National Forest. Thanks also to the Internet, without which I would not have considered leading such a long-distance cooperative project (it saved thousands of $$$) in phone bills)! About half of the initial inquiries for information came in through the Internet as well.
Bald Mountain Rogaine was a perfect finale to the 1995 "Washington State Orienteering Summertime Festival" (or WSOSF for those acronymically inclined) which began with the Bullfrog Flats A-meet and included Canoe-O, Poker-O, a Mt. Rainier area hike led by John Sincock and a Mt. St. Helens "hike" led by Dave Tallent. I believe concentrating such a variety of O-related activities into a 9-day portion on late July-August's usually dependable summertime will continue to attract worldwide visitors!

Congratulations and Thanks to all who participated!

Brits Beaten by a Whisker

John Britton

Despite extensive Karrimor experience, this was a step into the unknown, especially for Mark, who'd never been on his feet longer than 10 hours before, and who'd just picked up a back injury in the Scottish 6-Day. Lots of holiday activities got in the way of the usual sorts of preparation, but we were both pretty relaxed and optimistic at the start.
A 24-hour Rogaine is a very interesting competition - the start/finish "Hash House" is centrally placed, serving hot and delicious food throughout the event. At 10am, maps are dished out, pre-marked with controls worth 20,30,40,50,60,80,100 scattered all around. You have 2 hours to plan your strategy, and there's a mass start at 12 noon. You finish at 12 noon the next day, with prohibitive 10pts/min penalties for being late. It seems normal to plan a couple of loops, with a rest/refreshment/re-equip stop late on the first day. The big questions are what to do with the 9 hours of darkness, and how to pace yourselves.
In this event, controls were 21-27 (worth 20 each) 31-39 (worth 30), 41-49, 51-59, 61-65, 81-85, 101-104, 6 of them with water. There was no obvious theme to control values. Both days featured clear blue sky with blazing sun, making water a major issue (drinking from streams is dangerous, requiring iodine tablets and a 20-minute wait). Control placements varied between interesting places (cave, waterfall, Little Bald Mountain summit, cliff top), and the usual knolls, stream junctions, marshes etc. The advertised "lovely open runnable forest" was very rarely evident - mostly when you left the forest track network you took on assault-course stuff of different sizes and angles, with 2mph about the best you could hope for. Forest was usually thick, and several controls needed a bit of a hunt. The map was 1:36000 with 40ft contours, total size a neat 11x17inches. Highest point was 6000ft; lowest 1500ft, but there were few Karrimor-style huge uphill slogs. We were warned that not all the forest tracks on the map existed on the ground; and that new ones did exist.
Applying Karrimor speeds, adjusted for the extra time and darkness, we decided that getting all the controls was very possible, so we couldn't leave anything out. The North of the map had the vaguer contours, so we decided to go for that in the first day, leaving the steeper bits for darkness, and hopefully running the flatter stuff while fresh. We planned a loop with a few options towards the end, with a view to knocking off the North before dark, picking up torches and night clothes and then doing most of the South in the night.

Page 3

We started 24, 41, 56, discovering right away the differences in runnability and the trickiness of going straight to controls. Taking 55 from above should have been a simple case of identify-stream-and-follow-it-down. Pity the stream was not a bit obvious, and a major change in slope not mapped at all. 30 minutes wasted in a frantic hunt about half a mile early. Abandoned the search, and promptly stumbled on it on our way out. 39, 84, 61, 62, 47, 48 and 54 followed without incident, except picking the right knoll for 61 was more luck than judgement, and we got badly in need of water. Drinking heavily at the water control 54, we thought the Grand Plan was behind schedule but still feasible.
Fun and games getting down to 101 (foot of big waterfall), scrambling round a horrendous contour to the cave for 82 (10 mins wasted hunting for a control placed behind a bush 20 yards off the path) and a slow crossing to 49 meant the intended loop couldn't be done in daylight. We left the more Southerly ones for "later" and slogged back via 64, 58, 59, 33, 57 on tracks mixed with the worst forest I've ever seen (the climb to 57 will live in our nightmares forever).
We'd taken just over 8 hours for this loop - only a third of the event, and well over a third (980 out of 2320) of the points in the bag. We spent 45 minutes feasting; sorting out Petzls, clothes and fresh sweetie-bags. No foot damage, so no changes of shoes. The new Grand Plan featured a plausible-looking loop of the higher stuff to be done in the dark, followed by a smaller loop to do in the morning. Off we went in the pitch black, worried but pretty sure no other teams would have as much Night-O experience as we have.
Trivial 21, but very awkward 65. Getting this from below was hard - in the end, we had to relocate and get it from above. Across and what seemed a very long way upstream to 31. Change of plan took 44 next, as a long contour gave a decent attack route. We emerged from the forest only 100 yards from the control. Then big climb to 103 (top of the mountain), and track plus short plunge with crossed fingers to 35. Back to the track for easy 34. By now, it was getting pretty cold. For 53, there was nothing to do but set compass and hope to hit a big spur after half a mile. This worked perfectly. Hard work, but easy 104. 100 yards later, the expected tracks didn't materialize and we were back to guesswork. Bravely hit the meadow containing 83 off a bearing. Another aim and hope with careful watching contours for 52, none of which seemed to fit. Assorted recovery attempts along the lines of "if we are here and go South, we'll go down a bit then up" all failed as the expected never happened. Eventually, a serious sit-down and sort-out using the fact we could hear a stream turned us 180 degrees round, and some time later, we just managed to see the spur. Over an hour lost. 43 should have been a trivial trot down forest tracks, but the last kilometer of track wasn't there. Worse, the map bore little resemblance to the ground. After at least 30 minutes scouring the wrong area, dawn arrived, and we sat down for a rethink of what to do with the last 7 hours.
At long last, we abandoned plans for getting everything and tried to start optimizing points values. We gave up on 32/51/25, and thought we probably wouldn't reach 102/63/26 either. Pointing towards 81 we soon found a track/stream junction which showed exactly where 43 should be, so we went back and got it. Straight down track and steep spur to 81. Long track jog in cold early morning to 85. "Just nip up and down for 38" turned into a nightmare climb as 38 was placed a good 200ft too high. Slow but straightforward to 42, 45, 46, 37, with water becoming a factor again. No chance of further big points; no enthusiasm for straining for more little ones, and so back via 36, 23, 22 and just under 20 minutes to spare. Just a tidge more urgency in the last couple of hours would have given us 27 as well, but we just wanted to plod safely back to the food, drink and chairs.
In total, we probably ran for 2 hours; stood or sat for 1 hour; walked on tracks for 2 hours; and scrabbled round in the assault course for 19 hours. Of this, over 2 hours was wasted on 55, 52 and 43. Obviously, with a slightly dodgy 1:36000 map and controls in thick forest, nobody is going to get everything right, but this felt like one mistake too many. It's probably not coincidence that 52 and 43 were the last legs of a long night. Our Rogaine inexperience showed in setting off without a torch - this forced us to get back before dark, when it would have been more profitable to stick with the planned first loop and just extend it into the early part of the night. That's what the winners did. From a short chat, they did more or less the same as us to start with, but finished the first loop at 11pm with 200 more points; spent 1hr 45 resting and feeding; started a similar second loop, and accurately trimmed it by good judgment after dawn broke. With perfect hindsight, we could have made a better decision having finally punched 43 - 32/38/85/81/42 is shorter than 81/85/38/42 and worth 30 points more (but 32 looked a very tricky control to hit).
In short, Rogaine is a very good game, if you're fit and not deterred by darkness. We didn't enjoy most of the terrain, but we did enjoy the weather, the several scenic bits and the food. Other points - if we were to do another one, we'd set out with sufficient equipment to not need a Hash House stop; we worked pretty well together in the night - one leading, with the other checking the bearing was being followed properly; later in the night, we seemed to have a lot more bearing corrections to do, and mostly the same way - as if there's an innate tendency to circle which began to take over.

14th WIOL Season

Debbie Newell

Soon we will be starting the 14th edition of the Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League. WIOL has been quite successful in the past. Alumni of the league such as Eric Bone, Nick Bone, Ellen Boomer, and Lisa Nordlund, have all gone on to represent the U.S. at Junior, Senior, or Student World Championships. In addition, countless other students have gained confidence and problem-solving skills through orienteering.
In order to continue this success, we will need help in making the league run smoohtly. Specifically, we need help with course vetting, results, beginner instruction, or any miscellaneous assistance you can offer at meets. We also need help identifying school contacts who are willing to promote orienteering. If you can help in any of these areas, please call Debbie Newell at 206-488-7471.

State Championships

by Carl Moore

The Washington State Orienteering Championships will be contested at Cle Elum ridge on September 23rd and 24th. Nisqually Orienteers will host the event. Six courses are planned for both days. One must register in advance to get a pre-marked map.
It was exactly ten years ago that I ran in my first state championship meet at Cle Elum ridge. I saw alot of the woods that day and finished just under three hours. Since that time both the map and my O skills have been updated and improved. The woods and fields remain as freshly beautiful as ever. In many areas large, unmapped brush piles have helped concentrate the deadfall, making running, if not pleasurable, at least possible.
I designed the 1995 courses with Washington competitors in mind. Gary McCausland and Linda Moore had many helpful suggestions. Rick Hood has helped with technical arrangements. We are looking forward to seeing you at the meet. This issue of the Bearing contains an entry form.
[Day 1 of the State Championships will also be the U.S. part of the annual "Pig War" competition between B.C. and Washington clubs. -- Ed.]

Field Notes

  • An introductory class in orienteering skills and tactics will be offered through the UW Experimental College this Fall quarter. The class will cover topics from map reading and compass use to route choice and what to do when "lost". Beginning October 15th, the class will be held on Sundays through November 5th in Discovery Park. There will be two sections: one at 10:00am, the second at 12:30pm; each class meeting will be two hours long and will include outdoor exercises. The class will be taught by Mike Schuh, who has been teaching orienteering in the Seattle area (and elsewhere) for 15 years. To register for the class, contact the Experimental College at 543-4375. Questions on the class content may be directed to Mike at 783-3960.
  • Evan Custer, and the other 16 Bay Area OC members who came up for the A meet, sent me a nice note saying they had a super time and wanted to pass along a well done to SAMM. Evan hoped that they would see a nice turnout of Pacific Northwest orienteers at USOF Long O Champs this November that BOAC is hosting. (I know that Mac M., who won is class at the A meet, has his eye on a solid performance at the Long O and Debbie N. really wants to compete too but wants Eric B. to see if the latest poison oak treatment works well first, to ensure a pleasant weekend.) -- Rick Hood
  • Interested in attending the 1996 Swedish 5-day? When it comes to orienteering events, it's one of the biggest and one of the best! Contact Harvey Friedman for entry forms and more information.
  • This month our own Debbie Newell and Eric Bone came back from the World Orienteering Champs with tales of the competition. Rumor has it that both are fired up for some future international events. (Closer to home, they will be hosting a clinic for local orienteers sometime within the next year; look for details in a future Bearing or check the Hotline.) -- Rick Hood

Both Cascade and Sammamish clubs are raising their standard rates for events by $1, so it will be $3 for juniors, $4 for members and $5 for non-members from now on. John's suit will be mostly green to represent our woods, yellow to pay homage to the sun gods, and gray to represent the skies of the northwest. So for $45 you too can be "colored northwest"; call him at 462-9562 to get on the order list. -- Rick Hood

Symbolic Control Descriptions Explained

The control description sheet is the sheet of paper which describes the detail of where and how each orienteering control is hung. It's purpose is to describe the precise location of the control within the circle on the map, including the feature (often, but not always clear from the map circle), the exact location of the control relative to the feature, and the control's alphanumeric code.
If you've been to a few orienteering meets, you may have noticed that the advanced courses often have the control description sheet written in a symbolic form, rather than in plain English. This symbolic system was developed by the International Orienteering Federation to allow orienteers to compete on an equal basis, regardless of their knowledge of the local language. The symbolic descriptions also have the advantage of being compact, standard, and unambiguous. As you move onto the more advanced courses, you will find it advantageous to learn the symbolic description language; fortunately, it is very easy.
The symbolic description consists of eight columns (designated here A-H), with thick lines between column C and D, and between columns F and G. Each column has a precise meaning:
A: This column designates which control on the course is being described, starting with 1. This number matches the control number on the map. Sometimes a triangle is placed in this column to show where the start is.
B: This column has the unique alphanumeric code which is located on the control marker itself. Before punching a control, you should check this number against the control to confirm you are at the correct control.
C: This column indicates which of several similar features the control is located at. If there is only one feature of the correct type in or near the circle on the map, this column is left blank. Usually the

Page 6

symbol is an arrow, designating a direction: an arrow pointing up indicates the northern-most feature in the circle.
D: The most important column; this designates which feature the control is at. There are numerous symbols that can be placed in this column, from the common "reentrant" to the much less common "anthill". The most common symbols are easy to recognize, because the symbols mimic the standard map symbols as much as possible. This isn't always possible, however, because the map is in color, which the descriptions are black and white. For example, on the map a small knoll is a brown dot, and a boulder is a black dot. On the description sheet, a dot indicates a knoll, while a triangle indicates a boulder. (Confusingly, though, a black triangle on the map is a boulder cluster or field, which have different symbolic descriptions). It's worth taking a little time to familiarize yourself with the most common of the column D symbols; if you see one you don't recognize at a meet, just ask around and you'll find out; gradually you'll learn them all.
E & F: These two columns describes the appearance of the feature more precisely, and are only occasionally used. Column E is used for common adjectives, like "shallow," or for indicating the part of a linear feature, like "end" or "bend". Column F indicates the dimensions of a feature, always measured in meters. This is either a single number (indicating height), or two numbers separated by a cross (indicating length and width).
Sometimes, columns E and F are used differently, when the indicated feature is actually the intersection of two different features. In this case, column E has the symbol for either junction or crossing, and column F describes the other feature, using the same symbols as in column D. This is the only case when a column has multiple purposes; it is easy to distinguish the two cases by whether column F has a number or a symbol.
G: This column indicates the relative placement of the control to the feature described in columns C-F. Most of these symbols consist of a circle or corner designating the feature with the dot indicating the control's relative location. If the control is within the feature, the dot is inside the circle; if the control is outside the feature, the dot is outside the circle, and a tick mark intersecting the circle indicates a control right on the edge. The location of the dot indicates which side (for example, north-east). There are a few symbols for this column which don't fit this mold: for example, the symbols for "at the foot" or "on the top".
H: This column designates if there are any event related items at the control: usually drinking water or a meet official.

Be sure not to confuse columns C and G: C indicates the relation of the feature to nearby similar features; G indicates the relation of the control flag to the feature.
The top and bottom parts of the control description indicate a few things about the course: at the top is length and climb (length first); at the bottom is the distance to the finish.
Here is a short example of a control description sheet:

Samplesville Park
Course 1 4.5km 125m
[Start Triangle] [Junction/Crossing]
1 JX [West Arrow] [Re-entrant] [Shallow] [Upper part]
2 104 [Boulder] 2 [Southwest side] [Cup/Water]
3 412 [Path] [Crossing] [Wall]
[Navigate 200m] 200 > [Finish]

A rough English translation might be as follows: Course 1 is 4.5km long and has 125m of climb. The start is at a pit. The first control has code JX. It is in the western reentrant, which is shallow. The control is in the upper part. The second control has code 104. It is on the south-west side of a two meter boulder; drinking water will be there. The third control is code 412 and it at the intersection of a path and a stone wall. It is 200m from the last control to the finish.

Where and what are these Orienteering Clubs?

The Orienteering clubs listed here are independently administered non-profit organizations chartered by the United Stated Orienteering Federation. Sammamish primarily operates in the east-side (Bellevue and Redmond) area, while Cascade (the largest club) operates in the greater Seattle area. Ellensburg is centered in that town, Sacajawea is based in the Tri-Cities area, and the new Chuckanut O.C. is based in Bellingham. Husky Orienteering is for University of Washington students, faculty, and staff. Cascade and Sammamish clubs hold orienteering events throughout the year in the Puget Sound area, and eastward into the Cascade Mountains; the other clubs hold events in their areas. Membership is open to everyone, and entitles you to event discounts, club voting rights, and includes a subscription to Bearing 315, published six times per year.

What equipment do I need?
The only requirement is a compass. The best kind is a protractor-type with a clear plastic base so that you can see through it to the map underneath. Compasses are available for rent at orienteering events if you don't own one. At each event you will receive a detailed topographic map of the area, usually produced specifically for orienteering by the club. Long pants (light nylon is best, but jeans will do) and long-sleeve shirts are recommended because of sometimes dense underbrush. Jogging shoes or lightweight hiking boots are preferable; your feet may get wet and muddy, so a spare pair of socks and shoes are nice. Bring a watch so you can keep track of the time.

U. of Washington

Beginners Meet
July 23, 1995

While most of you were out of town at some of the major meets, those of us who remained behind for one reason or another decided to hold a local meet at the University of Washington. This event was touted as a Newcomers/Beginners meet.
Yet it was our intention to present courses for the more advance orienteers also. By all accounts Harvey did a great job at setting courses for all levels. He managed to send the even the runners most familiar with the campus on an interesting run through the campus.
Probably the most notable outcome of the meet was on course 4, the advanced course. Eric Bone and Bill Cusworth both ran fullout trying for personal best speeds on the mostly open running that the campus map presents. Both runners posted excellent times with few mistakes besting the times of all previous runners by 4 minutes. Yet it seems that possibly the biggest mistake was to not mark the construction fences on their maps and instead trusting to their knowledge of the campus layout. This became evident when the last runner on the course, Paul Magnus, came in with a faster time by 2 minutes. Other factors surely played a role as well in Paul's run, one factor surely being that the open nature of the campus that favors the roadrunner over the brush-buster. Also another factor that played a role in Paul's run was that he had previously run course 2 while carrying his daughter. Course 2 shared several controls with course 4 which may have given Paul a slight advantage over Eric and Bill. Never the less all three runners should be congratulated for posting such great runs.
Well this meet hopefully marks my return to a more active participation in orienteering. Over the last year or so I have attended very few meets due primarily to the birth of my son and work obligations. Therefore I decided that I should just dive back into orienteering by directing a local meet. The experience was as enjoyable as I remembered it to be and I would like to thank those of you who helped to make it so. First off, thanks to Harvey Friedman for setting such excellent courses and for helping with registration. Thanks to Rick and Linda Hood for running the start and finish table. Also thanks to Paul Magnus, Sandy Kish Miller, Bruce McAlister, and Bill Cusworth for being present to help orient the First-Time orienteers that we hoped would come to this event. Unfortunately only one newcomer came to the event (at least she did not lack competent instruction). And most especially, thanks to my wife Trish for spending time at a local 5K road race trying to interest the runners orienteering. -- Aaron Charlop

The Poker-O' went well at Hamlin Park in Seattle on Thursday evening, Aug. 17th. What is USOF?

Often in this newsletter, or in orienteering conversation, you will hear references to "yousoff," and you may have seen the magazine "Orienteering/North America." But why do they need so much money, you ask? We charge $10/year for local dues, so why should it cost an extra $25 to join an organization that doesn't seem to do much? The answer, of course, is that USOF does quite a bit.
The largest part of USOF dues goes for the O/NA subscription. This is not only a great source of information, it is well written and edited, and has many articles of general interest on health, exercise, and training. From time to time, we reprint articles from O/NA, and there are always copies available at major meets and club meetings. Most of the rest of USOF dues goes to pay for insurance. Although we, as individuals, might be willing to waive insurance, virtually all land owners, both public and private, require insurance as a precondition even to talking about using their land for orienteering. USOF members get a small rebate, about $2 per national meet, to help balance out the insurance, but, basically, USOF subsidizes our meets. Without USOF, I doubt that either Cascade or Sammamish could afford the insurance to hold another meet.
So we encourage you not only to join your local club, but to join and support USOF. The magazine along is worth the cost. You will also find as many opportunities to serve on national committees as there are to serve locally. USOF, like our local clubs, has no paid employees, and does not pay travel or per diem expenses for its officers. USOF, like Cascade, is an IRS section 501c(3) corporation. Which means that donations, including costs, are fully tax deductible. JOIN NOW!

block_index.text: BEARING 315 The Newsletter of Orienteering in the Pacific Northwest September, 1995 Cascade Orienteering
Terradan Landchild Benevolent Almighty Event Director

CROC's first major "O-vent" was an outstanding success with extensive, detailed planning, deep staff support from several clubs, a well-designed course and map, fantastic food, incredible scenery (sunrise on Mt. Rainier was especially spectacular) and even perfect weather! 73 enthusiastic participants formed 33 teams quite evenly split between the 24- and 12-hour sections. As much work as the staff put into this event, it's the participants who made it happen! The 1995 North American Championship title went to the Canadian Men's Masters team of Brian Chubb and Allan Stradeski of Kamloops, BC. Second place overall by a narrow margin (but first place in 24M) was a UK team, John Britton and Mark Seddon. John is currently "on loan" to CROC from the Manchester OC. However, the North American Championships were very nearly awarded to a European team! [John Britton writes about their experiences later in this issue. – Ed.] Complete official results are in the results section of this newsletter. For now, let's applaud those teams who made long trips to attend the Rogaine from: Belgium (with Michel Billaux, currently living in Pasco); yet another UK team (Pete Stagg & Jane Hancock); New York (Trysons); Maryland & VA (Brake & Organek); Louisville, KY (Greenlee & Bruce Moore); Indiana (Eric Grossman & Vlad Gusiatnikov); Idaho (Harbuck & Leveridge); Medford, OR (Joe Hallett & Danny Miller); Pt. Mugu and San Diego, CA (Ron Hudson & John Bennett); Edmonton, Alberta (Don Scott & Gene Brenda) and six more teams from British Columbia including one team of self described "ex-patriate" Australians (Glen Mair & Merideth Dally) who supplied the Canadian and Australian flags! The course itself was well-designed providing many different route choices; nothing was really obvious. As a safety precaution, all teams left a copy of their planned route on a black and white copy of the course map. There were a total of 48 controls ranging from 20 to 100 points depending upon difficulty, distance and point density. The Britton/Seddon team came closest at 41 to getting them all. Two controls were especially difficult: #32 on a spur and Boulder Cave. Bob is giving credit to all who reported the Cave control "missing"; actually the marker was simply "offset" which we should have noted on the description sheet. Although all of the participants at the pre-Start briefing loudly expressed their goal of having a fun Rogaine, some were also serious about finding controls! The most competitive teams ("elites") averaged about 100 points per hour, experienced teams, 60 points per hour and beginner teams, 30 points per hour, in both the 12- and 24-hours sections. The 12-hour section was any 12 hours out of the noon Saturday to noon Sunday time period. To gain night navigation experience, the 12-hour teams were also offered a "freebie": they could go out at dark and find one control and return while "off the clock" and not use any of their allotted 12 hours. Four teams took advantage of the offer and enjoyed "real Rogaining". Most were too tired from traversing the rigorous terrain. I rushed through the staff recognitions at the Awards Ceremony; I meant to put each staff in the spotlight and embarrass them one at a time but didn't. So here they are:
Bob Reddick (SAMM): The ROGAINE-MEISTER, who sparked the original inspiration and gave yeoman service as consultant, field-checker, timer and results coordinator, control pick-up.
Les Stark (CROC): Course designer, cartographer, field-checker, control setter, water crew chief, control picker, T-shirt design & ordering... (with help from his wife, Beth).

Copyright © 1995, Cascade Orienteering Club. Permission is given to reproduce for free distribution for the promotion of orienteering. Bearing 315 is published six times each year by Cascade Orienteering Club.
Of course, Less, Bob & I coordinated on dozens of details before the Rogaine! Also, the following folks all pitched in for much more than the jobs I have listed:
Mal Harding (CROC): field-checking, control setting & pick-up, traffic control
Linda & Carl Moore (NISQ): vetters & course setters
Don Atkinson (COC): porta-potty procurement, asst. timer

Special Appreciation Award to Kent Verbeck (EOC) for the fantastic food service! Not only wonderful food, but also enough quantity to leave once-starving Rogainers groaning when he offered thirds & fourths! I think folks will be talking about Kent's BBQ for years! Special Appreciation Award to my lovely wife, Jill Miller (CROC) for her infinite patience with an anxious, inexperienced and nuts Event Director during this past year of planning and development. Jill also waited on me hand and foot all during the event, finding things I had misplaced, making sure I ate and slept and making it possible for me to focus! Jill re-learned lashing and constructed the washstands and pitilators and did the horrible job of attaching reflective strips onto the CROC control markers with that nasty water-proof glue! I also appreciate her sacrifice of her vacation time to help me set up camp the week before the event, include what should have been our 1st Anniversary honeymoon trip! Thanks also to additional field-checkers Dave Tallent & Debbie Newell (COC). Do you notice the "cross-fertilization effect"? The Rogaine was officially a joint effort of the CROC and SAMM clubs, but members of EOC, NISQ, and COC were also on staff. It just goes to show what cooperation can do for you! Thanks also to our sponsors REI, PowerBar, BioZone, and the Naches District, Wenatchee National Forest. Thanks also to the Internet, without which I would not have considered leading such a long-distance cooperative project (it saved thousands of $$$) in phone bills)! About half of the initial inquiries for information came in through the Internet as well. Bald Mountain Rogaine was a perfect finale to the 1995 "Washington State Orienteering Summertime Festival" (or WSOSF for those acronymically inclined) which began with the Bullfrog Flats A-meet and included Canoe-O, Poker-O, a Mt. Rainier area hike led by John Sincock and a Mt. St. Helens "hike" led by Dave Tallent. I believe concentrating such a variety of O-related activities into a 9-day portion on late July-August's usually dependable summertime will continue to attract worldwide visitors!
Congratulations and Thanks to all who participated!

Despite extensive Karrimor experience, this was a step into the unknown, especially for Mark, who'd never been on his feet longer than 10 hours before, and who'd just picked up a back injury in the Scottish 6-Day. Lots of holiday activities got in the way of the usual sorts of preparation, but we were both pretty relaxed and optimistic at the start. A 24-hour Rogaine is a very interesting competition - the start/finish "Hash House" is centrally placed, serving hot and delicious food throughout the event. At 10am, maps are dished out, pre-marked with controls worth 20,30,40,50,60,80,100 scattered all around. You have 2 hours to plan your strategy, and there's a mass start at 12 noon. You finish at 12 noon the next day, with prohibitive 10pts/min penalties for being late. It seems normal to plan a couple of loops, with a rest/refreshment/re-equip stop late on the first day. The big questions are what to do with the 9 hours of darkness, and how to pace yourselves. In this event, controls were 21-27 (worth 20 each) 31-39 (worth 30), 41-49, 51-59, 61-65, 81-85, 101-104, 6 of them with water. There was no obvious theme to control values. Both days featured clear blue sky with blazing sun, making water a major issue (drinking from streams is dangerous, requiring iodine tablets and a 20-minute wait). Control placements varied between interesting places (cave, waterfall, Little Bald Mountain summit, cliff top), and the usual knolls, stream junctions, marshes etc. The advertised "lovely open runnable forest" was very rarely evident - mostly when you left the forest track network you took on assault-course stuff of different sizes and angles, with 2mph about the best you could hope for. Forest was usually thick, and several controls needed a bit of a hunt. The map was 1:36000 with 40ft contours, total size a neat 11x17inches. Highest point was 6000ft; lowest 1500ft, but there were few Karrimor-style huge uphill slogs. We were warned that not all the forest tracks on the map existed on the ground; and that new ones did exist. Applying Karrimor speeds, adjusted for the extra time and darkness, we decided that getting all the controls was very possible, so we couldn't leave anything out. The North of the map had the vaguer contours, so we decided to go for that in the first day, leaving the steeper bits for darkness, and hopefully running the flatter stuff while fresh. We planned a loop with a few options towards the end, with a view to knocking off the North before dark, picking up torches and night clothes and then doing most of the South in the night.

We started 24, 41, 56, discovering right away the differences in runnability and the trickiness of going straight to controls. Taking 55 from above should have been a simple case of identify-stream-and-follow-it-down. Pity the stream was not a bit obvious, and a major change in slope not mapped at all. 30 minutes wasted in a frantic hunt about half a mile early. Abandoned the search, and promptly stumbled on it on our way out. 39, 84, 61, 62, 47, 48 and 54 followed without incident, except picking the right knoll for 61 was more luck than judgement, and we got badly in need of water. Drinking heavily at the water control 54, we thought the Grand Plan was behind schedule but still feasible. Fun and games getting down to 101 (foot of big waterfall), scrambling round a horrendous contour to the cave for 82 (10 mins wasted hunting for a control placed behind a bush 20 yards off the path) and a slow crossing to 49 meant the intended loop couldn't be done in daylight. We left the more Southerly ones for "later" and slogged back via 64, 58, 59, 33, 57 on tracks mixed with the worst forest I've ever seen (the climb to 57 will live in our nightmares forever). We'd taken just over 8 hours for this loop - only a third of the event, and well over a third (980 out of 2320) of the points in the bag. We spent 45 minutes feasting; sorting out Petzls, clothes and fresh sweetie-bags. No foot damage, so no changes of shoes. The new Grand Plan featured a plausible-looking loop of the higher stuff to be done in the dark, followed by a smaller loop to do in the morning. Off we went in the pitch black, worried but pretty sure no other teams would have as much Night-O experience as we have. Trivial 21, but very awkward 65. Getting this from below was hard - in the end, we had to relocate and get it from above. Across and what seemed a very long way upstream to 31. Change of plan took 44 next, as a long contour gave a decent attack route. We emerged from the forest only 100 yards from the control. Then big climb to 103 (top of the mountain), and track plus short plunge with crossed fingers to 35. Back to the track for easy 34. By now, it was getting pretty cold. For 53, there was nothing to do but set compass and hope to hit a big spur after half a mile. This worked perfectly. Hard work, but easy 104. 100 yards later, the expected tracks didn't materialize and we were back to guesswork. Bravely hit the meadow containing 83 off a bearing. Another aim and hope with careful watching contours for 52, none of which seemed to fit. Assorted recovery attempts along the lines of "if we are here and go South, we'll go down a bit then up" all failed as the expected never happened. Eventually, a serious sit-down and sort-out using the fact we could hear a stream turned us 180 degrees round, and some time later, we just managed to see the spur. Over an hour lost. 43 should have been a trivial trot down forest tracks, but the last kilometer of track wasn't there. Worse, the map bore little resemblance to the ground. After at least 30 minutes scouring the wrong area, dawn arrived, and we sat down for a rethink of what to do with the last 7 hours. At long last, we abandoned plans for getting everything and tried to start optimizing points values. We gave up on 32/51/25, and thought we probably wouldn't reach 102/63/26 either. Pointing towards 81 we soon found a track/stream junction which showed exactly where 43 should be, so we went back and got it. Straight down track and steep spur to 81. Long track jog in cold early morning to 85. "Just nip up and down for 38" turned into a nightmare climb as 38 was placed a good 200ft too high. Slow but straightforward to 42, 45, 46, 37, with water becoming a factor again. No chance of further big points; no enthusiasm for straining for more little ones, and so back via 36, 23, 22 and just under 20 minutes to spare. Just a tidge more urgency in the last couple of hours would have given us 27 as well, but we just wanted to plod safely back to the food, drink and chairs. In total, we probably ran for 2 hours; stood or sat for 1 hour; walked on tracks for 2 hours; and scrabbled round in the assault course for 19 hours. Of this, over 2 hours was wasted on 55, 52 and 43. Obviously, with a slightly dodgy 1:36000 map and controls in thick forest, nobody is going to get everything right, but this felt like one mistake too many. It's probably not coincidence that 52 and 43 were the last legs of a long night. Our Rogaine inexperience showed in setting off without a torch - this forced us to get back before dark, when it would have been more profitable to stick with the planned first loop and just extend it into the early part of the night. That's what the winners did. From a short chat, they did more or less the same as us to start with, but finished the first loop at 11pm with 200 more points; spent 1hr 45 resting and feeding; started a similar second loop, and accurately trimmed it by good judgment after dawn broke. With perfect hindsight, we could have made a better decision having finally punched 43 - 32/38/85/81/42 is shorter than 81/85/38/42 and worth 30 points more (but 32 looked a very tricky control to hit). In short, Rogaine is a very good game, if you're fit and not deterred by darkness. We didn't enjoy most of the terrain, but we did enjoy the weather, the several scenic bits and the food. Other points - if we were to do another one, we'd set out with sufficient equipment to not need a Hash House stop; we worked pretty well together in the night - one leading, with the other checking the bearing was being followed properly; later in the night, we seemed to have a lot more bearing corrections to do, and mostly the same way - as if there's an innate tendency to circle which began to take over.

Soon we will be starting the 14th edition of the Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League. WIOL has been quite successful in the past. Alumni of the league such as Eric Bone, Nick Bone, Ellen Boomer, and Lisa Nordlund, have all gone on to represent the U.S. at Junior, Senior, or Student World Championships. In addition, countless other students have gained confidence and problem-solving skills through orienteering. In order to continue this success, we will need help in making the league run smoohtly. Specifically, we need help with course vetting, results, beginner instruction, or any miscellaneous assistance you can offer at meets. We also need help identifying school contacts who are willing to promote orienteering. If you can help in any of these areas, please call Debbie Newell at 206-488-7471.

The Washington State Orienteering Championships will be contested at Cle Elum ridge on September 23rd and 24th. Nisqually Orienteers will host the event. Six courses are planned for both days. One must register in advance to get a pre-marked map. It was exactly ten years ago that I ran in my first state championship meet at Cle Elum ridge. I saw alot of the woods that day and finished just under three hours. Since that time both the map and my O skills have been updated and improved. The woods and fields remain as freshly beautiful as ever. In many areas large, unmapped brush piles have helped concentrate the deadfall, making running, if not pleasurable, at least possible. I designed the 1995 courses with Washington competitors in mind. Gary McCausland and Linda Moore had many helpful suggestions. Rick Hood has helped with technical arrangements. We are looking forward to seeing you at the meet. This issue of the Bearing contains an entry form. [Day 1 of the State Championships will also be the U.S. part of the annual "Pig War" competition between B.C. and Washington clubs. -- Ed.]

An introductory class in orienteering skills and tactics will be offered through the UW Experimental College this Fall quarter. The class will cover topics from map reading and compass use to route choice and what to do when "lost". Beginning October 15th, the class will be held on Sundays through November 5th in Discovery Park. There will be two sections: one at 10:00am, the second at 12:30pm; each class meeting will be two hours long and will include outdoor exercises. The class will be taught by Mike Schuh, who has been teaching orienteering in the Seattle area (and elsewhere) for 15 years. To register for the class, contact the Experimental College at 543-4375. Questions on the class content may be directed to Mike at 783-3960.

The control description sheet is the sheet of paper which describes the detail of where and how each orienteering control is hung. It's purpose is to describe the precise location of the control within the circle on the map, including the feature (often, but not always clear from the map circle), the exact location of the control relative to the feature, and the control's alphanumeric code. If you've been to a few orienteering meets, you may have noticed that the advanced courses often have the control description sheet written in a symbolic form, rather than in plain English. This symbolic system was developed by the International Orienteering Federation to allow orienteers to compete on an equal basis, regardless of their knowledge of the local language. The symbolic descriptions also have the advantage of being compact, standard, and unambiguous. As you move onto the more advanced courses, you will find it advantageous to learn the symbolic description language; fortunately, it is very easy. The symbolic description consists of eight columns (designated here A-H), with thick lines between column C and D, and between columns F and G. Each column has a precise meaning: A: This column designates which control on the course is being described, starting with 1. This number matches the control number on the map. Sometimes a triangle is placed in this column to show where the start is. B: This column has the unique alphanumeric code which is located on the control marker itself. Before punching a control, you should check this number against the control to confirm you are at the correct control. C: This column indicates which of several similar features the control is located at. If there is only one feature of the correct type in or near the circle on the map, this column is left blank. Usually the

symbol is an arrow, designating a direction: an arrow pointing up indicates the northern-most feature in the circle. D: The most important column; this designates which feature the control is at. There are numerous symbols that can be placed in this column, from the common "reentrant" to the much less common "anthill". The most common symbols are easy to recognize, because the symbols mimic the standard map symbols as much as possible. This isn't always possible, however, because the map is in color, which the descriptions are black and white. For example, on the map a small knoll is a brown dot, and a boulder is a black dot. On the description sheet, a dot indicates a knoll, while a triangle indicates a boulder. (Confusingly, though, a black triangle on the map is a boulder cluster or field, which have different symbolic descriptions). It's worth taking a little time to familiarize yourself with the most common of the column D symbols; if you see one you don't recognize at a meet, just ask around and you'll find out; gradually you'll learn them all. E & F: These two columns describes the appearance of the feature more precisely, and are only occasionally used. Column E is used for common adjectives, like "shallow," or for indicating the part of a linear feature, like "end" or "bend". Column F indicates the dimensions of a feature, always measured in meters. This is either a single number (indicating height), or two numbers separated by a cross (indicating length and width). Sometimes, columns E and F are used differently, when the indicated feature is actually the intersection of two different features. In this case, column E has the symbol for either junction or crossing, and column F describes the other feature, using the same symbols as in column D. This is the only case when a column has multiple purposes; it is easy to distinguish the two cases by whether column F has a number or a symbol. G: This column indicates the relative placement of the control to the feature described in columns C-F. Most of these symbols consist of a circle or corner designating the feature with the dot indicating the control's relative location. If the control is within the feature, the dot is inside the circle; if the control is outside the feature, the dot is outside the circle, and a tick mark intersecting the circle indicates a control right on the edge. The location of the dot indicates which side (for example, north-east). There are a few symbols for this column which don't fit this mold: for example, the symbols for "at the foot" or "on the top". H: This column designates if there are any event related items at the control: usually drinking water or a meet official.

Be sure not to confuse columns C and G: C indicates the relation of the feature to nearby similar features; G indicates the relation of the control flag to the feature. The top and bottom parts of the control description indicate a few things about the course: at the top is length and climb (length first); at the bottom is the distance to the finish. Here is a short example of a control description sheet:

A rough English translation might be as follows: Course 1 is 4.5km long and has 125m of climb. The start is at a pit. The first control has code JX. It is in the western reentrant, which is shallow. The control is in the upper part. The second control has code 104. It is on the south-west side of a two meter boulder; drinking water will be there. The third control is code 412 and it at the intersection of a path and a stone wall. It is 200m from the last control to the finish.

Where and what are these Orienteering Clubs?

The Orienteering clubs listed here are independently administered non-profit organizations chartered by the United Stated Orienteering Federation. Sammamish primarily operates in the east-side (Bellevue and Redmond) area, while Cascade (the largest club) operates in the greater Seattle area. Ellensburg is centered in that town, Sacajawea is based in the Tri-Cities area, and the new Chuckanut O.C. is based in Bellingham. Husky Orienteering is for University of Washington students, faculty, and staff. Cascade and Sammamish clubs hold orienteering events throughout the year in the Puget Sound area, and eastward into the Cascade Mountains; the other clubs hold events in their areas. Membership is open to everyone, and entitles you to event discounts, club voting rights, and includes a subscription to Bearing 315, published six times per year.

What equipment do I need? The only requirement is a compass. The best kind is a protractor-type with a clear plastic base so that you can see through it to the map underneath. Compasses are available for rent at orienteering events if you don't own one. At each event you will receive a detailed topographic map of the area, usually produced specifically for orienteering by the club. Long pants (light nylon is best, but jeans will do) and long-sleeve shirts are recommended because of sometimes dense underbrush. Jogging shoes or lightweight hiking boots are preferable; your feet may get wet and muddy, so a spare pair of socks and shoes are nice. Bring a watch so you can keep track of the time.

Late spring rains kept this high prairie somewhat green and blooming. Burr Road's topography and vegetation continue to amaze and occasionally delight foot travelers. The courses were designed to minimize the climb and mix long and short legs. Although we claim that if given a map we are magic, it appears that the magic doesn't start until we get to the start. While most of you were out of town at some of the major meets, those of us who remained behind for one reason or another decided to hold a local meet at the University of Washington. This event was touted as a Newcomers/Beginners meet. Yet it was our intention to present courses for the more advance orienteers also. By all accounts Harvey did a great job at setting courses for all levels. He managed to send the even the runners most familiar with the campus on an interesting run through the campus. Probably the most notable outcome of the meet was on course 4, the advanced course. Eric Bone and Bill Cusworth both ran fullout trying for personal best speeds on the mostly open running that the campus map presents. Both runners posted excellent times with few mistakes besting the times of all previous runners by 4 minutes. Yet it seems that possibly the biggest mistake was to not mark the construction fences on their maps and instead trusting to their knowledge of the campus layout. This became evident when the last runner on the course, Paul Magnus, came in with a faster time by 2 minutes. Other factors surely played a role as well in Paul's run, one factor surely being that the open nature of the campus that favors the roadrunner over the brush-buster. Also another factor that played a role in Paul's run was that he had previously run course 2 while carrying his daughter. Course 2 shared several controls with course 4 which may have given Paul a slight advantage over Eric and Bill. Never the less all three runners should be congratulated for posting such great runs. Well this meet hopefully marks my return to a more active participation in orienteering. Over the last year or so I have attended very few meets due primarily to the birth of my son and work obligations. Therefore I decided that I should just dive back into orienteering by directing a local meet. The experience was as enjoyable as I remembered it to be and I would like to thank those of you who helped to make it so. First off, thanks to Harvey Friedman for setting such excellent courses and for helping with registration. Thanks to Rick and Linda Hood for running the start and finish table. Also thanks to Paul Magnus, Sandy Kish Miller, Bruce McAlister, and Bill Cusworth for being present to help orient the First-Time orienteers that we hoped would come to this event. Unfortunately only one newcomer came to the event (at least she did not lack competent instruction). And most especially, thanks to my wife Trish for spending time at a local 5K road race trying to interest the runners orienteering. -- Aaron Charlop
